content.problemStatement:
[**a simple graph** is an undirected graph that has no loops (edges connected at both ends to the same vertex) and no more than one edge between any two different vertices.]

 -Retrieved from http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graph_(mathematics)

The road system at Byteland can be seen as a simple graph in which the vertices represented by the cities and the edges represented by the roads connected two different cities. Besides, the road system is connected it means people can travel between any two cities. It is decided by the president that two roads will be concurrently upgraded in the next month. Since it may take a couple of weeks to get the work done, two roads should be chosen so that the connectivity of the system is hold during the up-gradation process. More clearly, people still can go between any pair of cities without using that two roads.


Which roads should be upgraded is not decided yet. There may be no way to choose such a two roads or may be many ways. Your mission is counting how many pair of roads **cannot** be chosen. Notice that two roads must be different and we consider only un-ordered pair ((1, 2) is the same as (2,1)).
constraints: There is at most one road between any two cities.
content.inputFormat: First line contains two integers N and M, representing the number of cities and roads respectively. Each of the next M lines contains two integers u and v, representing a road connecting the u-th city to the v-th city.
content.outputFormat: The number of ways to choose two roads that cannot be upgraded concurrently.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There are no pairs of roads that cannot be chosen for upgradation.
There are no pairs of roads that cannot be chosen for upgradation.
There are no pairs of roads that cannot be chosen for upgradation.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the concept of bridges in a graph. A bridge is an edge that, when removed, increases the number of connected components in the graph. We can use DFS to find all bridges in the graph and then count the number of pairs of bridges.
solution.timeComplexity: O(M + N) per test case, O(T * (M + N)) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) — for storing the graph and the visited array.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
# Uses input() or sys.stdin
# Handles all T test cases
import sys

# Define the structure for a graph edge
class Edge:
    def __init__(self, u, v):
        self.u = u
        self.v = v

# Define the structure for a graph
class Graph:
    def __init__(self, n, m):
        self.n = n
        self.m = m
        self.edges = []

# Function to find bridges in the graph
def findBridges(graph):
    low = [-1] * graph.n
    disc = [-1] * graph.n
    parent = [-1] * graph.n
    visited = [False] * graph.n
    time = 0
    bridgeCount = 0

    # Perform DFS to find bridges
    for i in range(graph.n):
        if not visited[i]:
            dfs(graph, i, low, disc, parent, visited, time, bridgeCount)

    # Print the number of pairs of bridges
    print(bridgeCount * (bridgeCount - 1) // 2)

# Function to perform DFS
def dfs(graph, u, low, disc, parent, visited, time, bridgeCount):
    visited[u] = True
    disc[u] = time
    low[u] = time
    time += 1

    # Iterate over all edges connected to u
    for edge in graph.edges:
        v = edge.v if edge.u == u else edge.u
        if not visited[v]:
            parent[v] = u
            dfs(graph, v, low, disc, parent, visited, time, bridgeCount)
            low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
            if low[v] > disc[u]:
                bridgeCount[0] += 1
        elif v != parent[u]:
            low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v])

# Read the input
t = int(input())

while t > 0:
    n, m = map(int, input().split())

    graph = Graph(n, m)

    # Read the edges
    for _ in range(m):
        u, v = map(int, input().split())
        u -= 1
        v -= 1
        graph.edges.append(Edge(u, v))

    findBridges(graph)

    t -= 1
